question: A player has great dribble moves during drills but keeps losing the ball in real games when a defender pressures them. What is most likely the real issue?
raw_answer: Most likely the real issue is that they haven't really either they are working on dribble moves that aren't very at game speed and so when they have to go at game speed they're rushing themselves or the issues that they don't have great ball control because it is really good to be able to use a lot of dribble moves to be able to do a crossover in and out a through the legs but being able to control the ball being able to direct where the ball goes and just dribbling it in place while using your off hand to kind of swipe away from Defenders is very important and that can also really deter you in a real game when there are people pressuring you and then obviously there's that psychological perspective as well if you get really scared of that Defender and he starts pressuring you you obviously will lose the ball and one of the biggest things you have to do here is really put yourself in these game situations so get Defenders that are going to pressure you and get comfortable in that uncomfortable environment.

question: A player practices shooting every day but their shot still feels inconsistent in games. What would you investigate first?
raw_answer: I would investigate how they catch the ball I think a lot of people don't understand that shooting isn't just your form and how you follow through but it's actually how you catch the ball this player might be catching the ball and not a shooting stance and he might be rushing himself in the shot because of that and often his shot timing and also just completely messing up that shot and what that does is that causes the shot to be more inaccurate and more inconsistent.

question: A player can finish well on their dominant side but struggles badly when attacking the opposite side of the rim. What is likely causing this?
raw_answer: What is likely causing this issue is that a lot of young developing players especially are not really used to shooting or to be doing layups on the opposite side of their dominant hand and what does what this does is this takes away a level of confidence in their left hand and also they just don't have enough practice and so something that you you should really do is focus on mikan drills which are basically just drills where you work on both hands and both feet in order to score around the rim.

question: A player moves quickly but constantly gets blocked at the rim. What are the most common underlying mistakes causing this?
raw_answer: Yeah at the end of the day, basketball is a five on five sport and not a one on one sport and this player is probably getting past a the initial defender but when it comes to help defense he is not able to go up strong or aggressively and that makes him get blocked and there are a lot of things that you can do one of the biggest things that you can do is use floaters and use space around the room and not just under the rim and another thing that you can do is obviously make sure that you go up aggressively so go off to feet and make sure that you go up with one hand and you go really high up you have to really be aware of those help Defenders and make sure that those help Defenders aren't going to block your shot and you know where they are and sometimes even though you can get past that first offender it is okay to not drive in when there are two or three big guys and then the court and it is also okay to not just shoot the ball you can drive in get these help Defenders to collapse and then kick it out to things to get an open three.

question: A player can dribble well standing still but loses control when moving at full speed. What skill gap does that reveal?
raw_answer: What that shows is that this Defender does a lot of drills that are very static and don't require him to move around and  also it shows that this Defender doesn't know what to do when he has to think about many many different things other than just driven the basketball and what I would say to this player is that first on when you're doing your static drills don't look at the basketball while you're dribbling look up at the floor look at the wall use a tennis ball and put it and catch it up and down that's a really good strategy and then also do a lot of drills are more Dynamic so move around the court dribble around and always have your head up if you're looking down at the ball then when there's a Defender at you you're only going to look down and you're going to just be dribbling around and have no clue where you're going.

question: A player practices a lot but never seems to improve during actual games. What could be going wrong in their training approach?
raw_answer: I think one of the biggest things that is very overlooked in basketball is the fact that basketball is just as much as a physical game as it is a mental game and as much as you can practice a lot with your skills your shooting your your court Vision your IQ there is a lot to do with the mental game and how you approach the game itself and if this player is not being able to approve in these actual games then maybe there might be a psychological barrier that is not allowing him to approve he might be scared he might be living in fear and all of those things contribute to him not being able to improve and not be able to try out these new skills that he's learning

question: A beginner player asks what skill they should focus on first: shooting, ball handling, or finishing. How would you decide?
raw_answer: I would say to start out first I think shooting is the best way to start that just because you don't need to be able to dribble the ball really well or finish the the ball really well but if you could shoot really well you could just stand in the corner and just shoot shots whenever you're open and honestly this archetype of basketball like Klay Thompson or like a three indeed sort of shooter is a very very strong archetype and and so I think that shooting is a very very important skill to have and probably one of the most important skills to have in this modern generation of basketball

question: A player only has 30 minutes per day to practice. What should they prioritize for the fastest improvement?
raw_answer: I would say that they should prioritize everything so I would split those 30 minutes into 10 minute chunks 10 minutes being like form shooting 10 minutes be ball handling and then 10 minutes be finishing around the rim and I think this will allow them to compound over time into a very accomplished and very well-rounded player.

question: A player wants to improve their scoring ability. Should they focus more on ball handling, finishing, or shooting first?
raw_answer: If a player wants to improve their scoring ability so just scoring raw points I think shooting is the most important because you could just catch the ball from deep and just shoot it and if you can consistently make that you automatically become one a threat and you automatically get a lot of points so I think that one of the biggest things that you can do to improve your scoring ability is learning how to shoot the basketball from very far away.

question: A young player wants to learn flashy dribble moves early. Is that a good idea or not?
raw_answer: I mean I'm not against anyone learning fashionable you you really have to understand what's good for you and what's not that Kyrie Irving has a lot of flashy turbo moves and he's been doing that from a very young age and so he's being very successful in it but then you have Julian Newman who had all these flashes removed and has gone nowhere with it so I think it's not about it being a good idea or not but it being impactful to your game or not so if these flashes removed are something that you can utilize really well like you could do the Sham God really well and you get past Defenders pretty well with it then by all means do it and practice it but I would also say that the fundamentals are always really important.

question: A player wants to get better at beating defenders off the dribble. What should they train first?
raw_answer: I think that they should train their footwork they should learn how to or not even learn but improve their fast twitch muscle fibers that account for explosiveness a lot of these plays that you see where a player gets past the defender isn't so much to do with the flashiness of their move or how well they do the move as it is to do with how fast they're fast with fibers are obviously a misdirection is very important especially in the NBA there are many many misdirections that happen but the biggest thing that you should train first is your footwork is your fest with social fibers that allow you to be insanely explosive so you can just blow by Defenders without even having to do crazy devil moves and be very ball heavy.

question: If someone could only practice one drill for ball handling, what type of drill would give the best long-term improvement?
raw_answer: I think one type of drill that's really good for ball handling is using two basketballs or a basketball and tennis ball cuz again when you want to learn ball handling you want to be second nature and so I would really recommend that you do something where they're two activities done at once where you have to focus on the first activity and you cannot focus on dribbling and what that allows you to do is that it allows you to put that ball handling into second nature and so when you're in a game you don't have to worry about where the ball's going that's already happening and you can worry about the like the 20 other things that are going on on the court.

question: Which matters more for ball handling: tight control or speed, and why?
raw_answer: Tight control matters more than speed and here's why take for example luka doncic does not dribble the ball really really fast he does not do anything crazy flashy but he has very very tight control over the ball he is very good at making sure the ball is where he wanted to be in his pocket where no one can reach him and he's really good at using his off time he is dangerously slow and what that allows him to do is to be able to make great great plays so before anything I think control over the ball is very important and after you get control naturally speed will come.

question: Is it better to practice dribbling stationary or while moving at game speed?
raw_answer: I think it is really important to practice dribbling while moving at game speed because when you are even just in general when you're working out and playing basketball you should always be moving at game speed and getting your body used to that uncomfortable environment to the point where it becomes comfortable in that uncomfortable environment and what that will allow you to do is that when you are at in a game you'll be very comfortable dribbling and you'll be a very comfortable doing a lot of the things that are needed to be done at a game and so you can focus on a lot of other different things that you might not be able to focus on if you weren't comfortable moving at game speed.

question: Is it more important to train weak-hand control or advanced dribble moves early?
raw_answer: I think it is more important to train we can control early on because again very young developing players are not very nuanced at using their non-dominant hand and that can be a huge weakness in their game early on and so making sure that you have weekend control whether that be with the ball when you're dribbling or whether that be with layups is very important and getting down Advanced double moves aren't really that important early on.

question: Should a player practice more individual drills or live 1-on-1 situations?
raw_answer: to be honest there is no clear set rule on what should be done and what shouldn't obviously there should be a balance just doing individual drills or just doing live 101 situations will not help you but both of them have their benefits like with individual drills you're going to get to practice your fundamentals more and get better while with one-on-one situations you can put those fundamental skills or those drills that you have practiced into a real world scenario into real life situation and see what works with your with how you play basketball and what doesn't and so both of them are very important and there is no clear set rule on what is better and what isn't.

question: A player has limited time to train. Should they focus on many different drills or repeating a small number of drills?
raw_answer: Obviously the answer is to repeat a small number of roads by doing many many different rows all you're doing is becoming a jack of all trades and a master none and you should be focusing on a very small number of drills that Target a very fundamental level of how you play basketball and get really good at them and what that'll allow you to do is be able to apply them to game specific situations where it just becomes like it just becomes very natural.

question: Is it better for a developing player to work on perfect mechanics or game-like reps first?
raw_answer: So this question is very nuanced and I think the answer is that both of them mean the same thing perfect mechanics and game like reps are the same if you have perfect mechanics or if you want to focus on perfect mechanics there are going to be game like that is what perfect mechanics are like so there is no either or if you want to perfect your mechanics they have to be game like reps and that means that you have to be doing them with very high intensity you have to be doing them thinking that there's a defender in your face and you have to be doing them with that psychological level of that you're in a game.

question: A smaller player who isn’t very tall wants to score more effectively. What skills should they prioritize?
raw_answer: I think the main skill that they should prioritize is shooting because at the end of the day driving in even when you're short driving in is very very hard unless you're insanely politic and can jump very very high the best way to make an impact on the game is to be able to shoot the ball really well and so if you are a short player or a player that it lacks in size I think the biggest thing you can do is get really good at shooting and especially shooting from deep and what that what that does is it makes Defenders want to magnet onto you more and that allows you to be able to drive more freely and be able to do those mid-range jumpers those floaters and different things that you can put in your bag.

question: A player is very athletic but has poor technical fundamentals. How should they structure their training?
raw_answer: They should structure their training with a very fundamental approach so that means get really good at shooting from very close and get really good at understanding their form shooting and how to shoot with perfect form they should know how to dribble the ball so that means using the pads of your fingers and not the Palm they should be able to understand how to kind of do lay up so you don't using those like a two-step framework being able to do a jump step being able to do a hop step these are all very fundamental things that a player learns on very very early into their basketball career and these are things that that player would want to learn obviously his athleticism was still outrank his ability and that will still keep happening until his ability catches up but with athleticism you can't really do much with athleticism unless you have that ability so it is very important to focus on that fundamental ability much more than that athleticism at that point of your life.

question: A beginner player is overwhelmed by the number of skills in basketball. What is the simplest starting framework you would give them?
raw_answer: There are a lot of skills but it is not about the skills as much as it is about the fundamentals this is a very important concept that a lot of people have to understand and it is the fact of the matter that if you want to be good at a game you have to be very very fundamental in the things that you do and that means you should be able to dribble the ball and control the ball really well that means that you should be able to use your off hand to swipe away Defenders that means you should be able to attack with really really good angles that means that you should be able to shoot from deep and from close that means you should be able to pull up jumpers really well these are all very very fundamental things in the game of basketball that you have to be very nitpicky about and you have to be almost perfect if you really want to do something with the game.

question: A player has decent skills but lacks confidence during games. What could be causing that?
raw_answer: What could be causing that is obviously the psychological standpoint in basketball is very important and and him getting in his own head is something that could be caused by many many reasons and and honestly I would need to know more about that player but some of the reasons that I know of today are you know fear of failure you could just not be confident in yourself I mean you can have a lot of skills but there is a difference from having a lot of skills and being very confident in yourself to be able to perform those skills and there's also the fact that maybe you might not have like a pregame thing to do or maybe you're very prone to trash talk so there are a lot of things that could be happening that take away from the psychological point of the game and you just have to really think about yourself internally and I think there's a book called a 10 minute toughness 10 minute mental toughness and I think that's a really good book to read in order to understand how to be you know mentally strong and have that confidence during games.

question: A player has strong ball handling but struggles to create separation from defenders. What might they be missing?
raw_answer: I think that player might be suffering from doing a lot of nothing so in other words they might be doing a bunch of dribble moves and not really getting anywhere with there because at the end of the day to get past the defender you don't need to be able to Rebel the ball really well what you need to be able to do is to be able to be very explosive and to be able to attack angles really well and be able to use your off hand really well and so this player might not be able to might not be that explosive they might not be able to attack angles that well they they might be doing a whole lot of things that really get them nowhere and so that's where that saying doing a whole lot of nothing but getting nowhere comes from and that's what this player might be struggling from.

question: When attacking a defender off the dribble, what are the most important things a player should be reading?
raw_answer: I honestly don't read the defender in front of me that much I mean one of the their two things that I think I would say one of them is spacing so how does the defender treat you as an offender so does he give you space to shoot a shot does he really play vacuum defense and once you read that you can understand what you can do with the ball and what you can do to explode past them or to get a shot up and the other thing you should be reading is where is there lead foot so usually a Defender has a Lead Foot that is slightly extended farther out towards you then they're back foot and that lead foot basically gives you an angle as to where to attack and if they're flat-footed that means that they don't have a lead foot then that usually tells you that you can shoot the ball.

question: What makes a dribble move actually effective against a defender rather than just looking impressive?
raw_answer: What makes it remove really effective is having a counter to that river move so when I do a dribble move and I try to get past someone what makes that dribble move really really effective is the fact that if that doesn't work because no drill move is going to work 100% of times you can counter that move really really fast and so that's something I think players should know is that when you do a move you shouldn't be expecting to get to the rim but what you should be expecting to do is either do a counter move or try to do something else more to get past them it's something that Kyrie Irving preaches a lot and I think it is something that a lot of players need to know is that when you're trying to get past the defender and when you trying to dribble moves always have the counters in your mind because these Defenders are going to learn they're going to learn how you play and they are going to try getting Pat in front of you they're trying to not make sure that they get juked out you know what I mean.

question: What are the most common mistakes players make when trying to beat someone off the dribble?
raw_answer: One of the most common mistakes that players make when they're trying to beat someone off the dribble is not attacking angles and that's very important is understanding what angle to attack at if you try going around the defender you're never going to beat them you have to think about it as going through them and that requires a little physicality and level of athleticism and that's something that LeBron does really well it's what a lot of very explosive players do really really well and very simple it's understanding angles and understanding to attack their hip it's understanding to get low and from there you can beat anyone off the dribble

question: Why do some players look great in practice but struggle during competitive games?
raw_answer: I think it's a very it has two sides to it so the one thing that they that could be going on is that in practice it's not always game speed and during a game obviously it's game speed so they're not used to that quick environment and what that that causes them to do is to rush themselves which cause them to lose the ball or make mistakes and the second thing is obviously that's logical barrier that when you're playing against another team you're playing an actual game you might be playing much worse because you are scared or you're fearful or you're just in your own mind.

question: What are the biggest mistakes players make when practicing basketball skills?
raw_answer: The biggest mistake that players make is not going against speed this is something that Steve Kerr told actually he told Anthony Edwards when he watched him practice and it's that when you are practicing your basketball skills you have to go at the same level of intensity that you play at a game that is the only way that you're going to improve and so one of the biggest mistakes that players make is obviously just not going at that same intensity is not going at the game speed and when you start doing that you'll see a lot more changes in your game .

question: If someone wants to improve as quickly as possible over the next three months, how should they structure their training?
raw_answer: They should structure their training if they want to improve as much as they can over the next 3 months one thing that they realize is that to put many many hours into the game of basketball to get better and two basketball is a very nuanced sport so you have to practice and learn a lot of things and the way I would structure their training is by one focusing on their athleticism so that means focusing on your fast which muscle fibers making sure you're fast your legs are very strong you can stay in a defensive stance for a very long time you also want to focus on your cardio you want to make sure you're not tired because if you're tired there's less oxygen in your brain there's less option in your brain you cannot make decisions nearly as quickly and then apart from athleticism you want to work on do your technical skills so your ball handling you're off the dribble shooting your how you pick up the ball how do you lay up how you get around the court all of this is very important and then obviously you're shooting which is another part so I would structured in three parts athletic training then your technical you're fundamental skills and then you're shooting and you would probably have to do three different workouts every day for the next three months if you really want to see crazy considerable improvements.

question: What is the most underrated skill in basketball development that players often neglect?
raw_answer: I think the most underrated skill in basketball development that players often neglect is playing the mind game and this is something I really respect NBA players for doing I remember I watched an interview Draymond Green was talking about how he was a rookie and he was playing as Kobe Bryant and Kobe Bryant was back in good dream on green up and he does a little fade gets Draymond Green and shoots the ball and he misses and Draymond Green means like yeah let's go and he starts talking trash and Kobe just smiled it and he's like you didn't do anything I just missed the shot I mean just see them mentality in that like the mentality that you need to have to be playing at a very high level is insane you have to be almost delusionally confident in yourself and you have to be playing and you have to be deliciously confident yourself while playing at a very very high level against very very good players and so that requires you to build this level of mentality that is almost impossible a lot of players can't do it and I think that's a very underrated skill in basketball development that players genuinely often neglect.
